1.synthesis: Moses, now a shepherd in the wilderness of Midian for forty years, leads the flock to Horeb — the mountain of God — and encounters the Angel of the LORD in a flame of fire from within a bush that burns without being consumed (vv.1–2). His curiosity draws him to look: 'I will turn aside and see this great sight, why the bush is not burned.' When God sees Moses turn aside, he calls him by name: 'Moses, Moses.' The double-name address — the same as Abraham at Moriah, Jacob at Peniel, Samuel in the sanctuary — marks the highest grade of divine summons. God identifies himself: 'I am the God of your father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob' (v.6). Moses hides his face, afraid to look at God. The divine commission follows: 'I have seen the affliction of my people … I know their sufferings … I have come down to deliver them … and to bring them up out of that land' (vv.7–8). Moses is sent. His objection — 'Who am I that I should go?' — receives the covenant answer: 'I will be with you.'
1.historical_context: Horeb (the Sinai range) is the mountain of God — 'the back of the desert' where the theophany occurs is geographically remote, the appropriate setting for a divine encounter outside normal human habitation. The burning bush (sneh, a thorny shrub, possibly acacia or bramble) is a common plant; its burning without consumption is the anomaly that arrests Moses. The Angel of the LORD who appears in the flame is identified in v.4 as YHWH himself and in v.6 as the God of the patriarchs — the Christophany (pre-incarnate Son) pattern the Fathers recognized. The theophany repeats the pattern of the patriarchal encounters: God appears unsought, in a liminal place, at a turning point of the story. The affliction God has 'seen' and 'heard' (vv.7, 9) uses the covenant-attention formula: God is not passive to Israel's suffering but actively attentive to it.
1.literary_structure: The burning bush narrative is structured in three acts: the anomaly and approach (vv.1–3), the divine self-identification and commission (vv.4–10), and Moses's first objection and divine reassurance (vv.11–12). The structure repeats across the entire call narrative of chs.3–4: objection, divine response, objection, divine response. God's speech in vv.7–10 is itself a structural reflection of the Exodus: God has seen → heard → known → come down → will bring up. The five verbs chart the movement from observation to action. The sign promised in v.12 — 'you shall serve God on this mountain' — is given after the fact, not before: Moses must go in faith before the sign confirms his mission.
1.theological_application: Gill on the Angel of the LORD in the burning bush: 'Not a created angel, but the Angel of God's presence and covenant, the eternal Word and Son of God; since he is afterwards expressly called Jehovah, and calls himself the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, which a created angel would never do.' Matthew Henry on 'I will be with you': 'This is sufficient to silence all objections … the presence of God with his servants assures them of protection in their dangers, direction in their difficulties, and success in their undertakings.' Moses's 'who am I?' is not false modesty but genuine inadequacy — the right posture before the divine commission. God does not answer the question about Moses's identity but about God's presence: the adequacy is not in the servant but in the One who sends.
1.christology: Stephen's sermon (Acts 7:30–35) identifies the bush-appearing figure as 'the angel who appeared to him in the bush' and the one who sent Moses as deliverer: 'this man God sent as both ruler and redeemer by the hand of the angel who appeared to him in the bush.' The pre-incarnate Son appears in the burning bush as he appeared to Abraham at Mamre and to Jacob at the Jabbok. John 8:58 — 'before Abraham was, I am' — connects the Exodus 'I AM' directly to Christ. The burning bush that is not consumed has been read by the Fathers as a figure of the incarnation: the divine nature united to human flesh without consuming or destroying it, the fire of deity dwelling within mortal form.

13.synthesis: Moses's second objection at the bush concerns divine identity: if the Israelites ask 'what is his name?', what shall I say? (v.13). The answer given is one of the most theologically weighty in the OT: 'I AM WHO I AM (ʾehyeh ʾăšer ʾehyeh). Say this to the people of Israel: "I AM has sent me to you"' (v.14). Then: 'Say this to the people of Israel: "YHWH, the God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, has sent me to you. This is my name forever, and thus I am to be remembered throughout all generations"' (v.15). The commission continues: go to the elders, tell them, go to Pharaoh and ask for a three-day journey into the wilderness. Pharaoh will not let Israel go, but God will strike Egypt with wonders, and Israel will leave with Egypt's silver and gold — a plundering of Egypt by those who leave it (vv.19–22).
13.historical_context: The divine name YHWH is derived from the Hebrew root hayah (to be, to become, to be present). The imperfect form ʾehyeh — 'I will be' or 'I am' (ongoing, not completed) — gives the name both ontological and relational weight: absolute, unconditioned being AND the one who will be present with Israel in their deliverance. In the ancient world, a deity's name was not a label but a disclosure of character and covenant relationship. The patriarchs knew and used the name YHWH (Genesis 15:7), but the Exodus context declares its full covenant meaning for the first time: the God who IS, who will BE present, who cannot fail because his being is his faithfulness. The three-day journey request was a standard ancient diplomatic opening — a modest first demand that could be escalated.
13.literary_structure: The name-revelation (vv.14–15) is the theological climax of the bush narrative. The two-stage response — 'I AM WHO I AM' (v.14) and then 'YHWH, the God of your fathers' (v.15) — gives both the inner meaning of the name and its historically specific form. 'This is my name forever, and thus I am to be remembered throughout all generations' (v.15) formally establishes YHWH as the covenant name for all subsequent Israelite worship. The prophecy of Egypt's plundering (vv.21–22) anticipates Exodus 12:35–36 — the Israelites leave with Egypt's wealth, fulfilling the Abraham covenant's promise that the oppressing nation would be judged and the oppressed would leave with great possessions (Genesis 15:14).
13.theological_application: Gill on 'I AM THAT I AM': 'This signifies the real being of God, his self-existence, and that he is the Being of beings; as also it denotes his eternity and immutability, and his constancy and faithfulness in fulfilling his promises, for it includes all time, past, present, and to come.' Matthew Henry: 'I AM THAT I AM — this explains his name Jehovah, and signifies, (1.) That he is self-existent; he has his being of himself … (2.) That he is eternal and unchangeable, and always the same … (3.) That he is incomprehensible; we cannot by searching find him out.' The name YHWH binds divine ontology to covenant faithfulness: God's existence is not a philosophical abstraction but the ground of his promise to Israel. He is the one who IS, and therefore can be trusted to do what he has said.
13.christology: Jesus's 'I am' statements in John's Gospel deliberately echo the Exodus name. In John 8:58 the claim 'before Abraham was, I am' (egō eimi) applies the divine name to himself, and the Jewish hearers understood the claim as divine self-identification — they took up stones. The seven 'I am' statements of John (bread of life, light of the world, door, good shepherd, resurrection and life, way and truth and life, the vine) each fill the name with specific redemptive content. The Angel in the burning bush who declared 'I AM WHO I AM' is the same one who would say 'I am the way and the truth and the life' (John 14:6). God's 'memorial-name throughout all generations' (v.15) is the name above every name to which every knee will bow (Philippians 2:9–10).
